bonsaiforest2 0.1.0

Statistical Specifications

Miriam Pedrera Gomez, Isaac Gravestock, and Marcel Wolbers

Source: vignettes/Statistical_Specifications.Rmd
Statistical_Specifications.Rmd

1 Introduction

The bonsaiforest2 package implements a flexible, two-step Bayesian framework for estimating subgroup treatment effects in randomized controlled trials (RCTs). The approach is designed to handle both isolated subgroup analyses and complex, overlapping subgroup structures while mitigating the risk of false-positive findings through Bayesian shrinkage.

The estimation process consists of two primary steps: 1. Model Fitting: Fitting a Bayesian conditional outcome model to the trial data. 2. Standardization: Deriving marginal treatment effect estimates for subgroups via G-computation (marginalizing over the covariate distribution within the subgroup).

2 Modeling Frameworks

The package supports two primary modeling approaches:

Feature One-Way Shrinkage Model Global Shrinkage Model
Scope Fit one model per each of the subgrouping variables Fit one model including all the subgrouping variables
Subgroups (\(K\)) \(K\) levels of the single variable. Total \(K = \sum_{j=1}^p l_j\) subgroups (overlapping).
Subject Indicators Only a single indicator \(s_{ik}=1\) per subject. \(p\) indicators \(s_{ik}=1\) per subject.

3 Outcome Model and Linear Predictor

The outcome model is a Bayesian regression model. bonsaiforest2 supports multiple endpoint types, expressing treatment effects through appropriate standard measures:

  • Continuous: Linear regression (Mean differences)
  • Binary: Logistic regression (Odds Ratios, OR)
  • Count: Negative Binomial regression (Rate Ratios, RR) — supports offset terms
  • Time-to-Event: Cox regression (Average Hazard Ratios, HR) — uses non-negative M-splines for baseline hazard

Let \(i = 1,\ldots , N\) denote subjects, \(z_i \in \{0, 1\}\) denote treatment assignment (0 = control, 1 = intervention), \(s_{ik}\) denote subgroup indicators, and \(u_{il}\) denote additional baseline covariates. The linear predictor \(LP_i\) for subject \(i\) is structured as follows:

\[LP_i = \alpha_0 + \beta_{0}z_i + \underbrace{\alpha_1 s_{i1}+\ldots + \alpha_K s_{iK} + \alpha_{K+1} u_{i1} +\ldots + \alpha_{K+L}u_{iL} }_{\substack{\text{prognostic effects including} \\ \text{main subgroup effects}}} + \underbrace{\beta_1 s_{i1} z_i +\ldots + \beta_K s_{iK}z_i}_{\substack{\text{predictive effects: subgroup-by-} \\ \text{treatment interactions}}}\]

Note: For Cox regression models, the intercept \(\alpha_0\) is excluded.

4 Parameter Grouping and Prior Distributions

To provide maximum flexibility, bonsaiforest2 categorizes regression coefficients (excluding the intercept and main treatment effect) into three distinct groups. The encoding and prior strategies differ across these groups:

Parameter Group Description Encoding Prior Strategy
Unshrunken Terms Typically prognostic effects, or predictive terms with strong a priori rationale (e.g., direct drug target biomarkers). Dummy encoding (baseline levels omitted to avoid overparameterization). Non-informative / Flat priors.
Shrunken Prognostic Terms Prognostic effects requiring regularization (e.g., due to a large number of terms relative to sample size). One-hot encoding (all levels included symmetrically). Shrinkage priors (exchangeable within this group).
Shrunken Predictive Terms Subgroup-by-treatment interactions. For one-way models, this is just the selected variable. For global models, it encompasses all interactions. One-hot encoding (all levels included symmetrically). Shrinkage priors (exchangeable within this group).

The package utilizes brms (and the Stan backend) to fit these models, allowing for highly flexible prior specifications.

4.1 Priors for One-Way Shrinkage

For one-way models where the number of subgroups is typically low, coefficients share a common normal prior distribution with a half-normal (HN) prior assigned to the standard deviation:

\[\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_K \sim N(0,\tau^2) \mbox{ with } \tau \sim HN(\phi)\]

The heterogeneity parameter \(\phi\) dictates the degree of shrinkage. As a conservative default, \(\phi = |\delta_{plan}|\) (where \(\delta_{plan}\) is the target effect size from the trial protocol) ensures that the a priori pairwise differences in treatment effects between subgroups lie between \(0.02\cdot|\delta_{plan}|\) and \(3.09\cdot |\delta_{plan}|\) with 90% probability.

4.2 Priors for Global Shrinkage

Because global models handle a larger number of subgroups (e.g., 10 to 30) we recommend using a Global-local shrinkage prior. bonsaiforest2 employs the Regularized Horseshoe prior as a default. This global-local shrinkage prior encourages sparsity for noise while maintaining fat tails to allow true, strong signals to escape shrinkage:

\[ \beta_k|\lambda_k, \tau, c \sim N(0,\tau^2\tilde{\lambda}_k^2)\mbox{ with } \tilde{\lambda}_k^2 = c^2 \lambda_k^2 / (c^2 + \tau^2 \lambda_k^2)\]

With hyper-priors: \[\tau\sim C^+(0,\tau_0^2), \; \lambda_k \sim C^+(0,1)\; (k=1,\ldots,K), \mbox{ and } c^2 \sim \textrm{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2,\nu s^2 /2)\]

  • Slab parameters (\(\nu\), \(s\)): Default choices are typically \(s=2\) and \(\nu=4\) for endpoints modeled on the log-scale, providing very weak regularization for the slab.
  • Global shrinkage parameter (\(\tau_0\)): Can be calibrated based on the expected magnitudes of regression coefficients and the planned overall treatment effect \(|\delta_{plan}|\).

5 Standardization (G-Computation)

Because the linear predictor conditions on covariates, the raw subgroup-by-treatment interaction terms represent conditional effects. bonsaiforest2 uses standardization to convert these into clinically interpretable marginal treatment effects.

The standardization process follows three steps: 1. Prediction: The fitted global model predicts potential outcomes for every subject \(i\) twice—once assuming hypothetical assignment to control (\(z_i=0\)) and once to intervention (\(z_i=1\)). For time-to-event data, full survival curves \(\hat{S}_{i,C}(t)\) and \(\hat{S}_{i,I}(t)\) are predicted. 2. Marginalization: Predicted potential outcomes (or survival curves) are averaged across all subjects within a specific subgroup, resulting in a marginal prediction for the subgroup under both treatment conditions. 3. Estimation: The subgroup treatment effect is calculated by contrasting the marginal intervention prediction against the marginal control prediction (e.g., yielding a marginal mean difference, OR, RR, or average HR).

Final treatment effect estimates are summarized using the median of the posterior draws, accompanied by a 95% credible interval (bounded by the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles).

On this page

Developed by Miriam Pedrera Gomez, Isaac Gravestock, Marcel Wolbers.
